questions.prompt: When you snap, go cold, over-explain, or agree to something you already know you will resent, when do you usually realise what's happening?

options.label: I rarely see it at all.
I usually see it much later.
I see it once I have calmed down.
I often see it while it is happening.
I can usually see it and choose differently.

questions.prompt: When you look at your ambition, standards, relationships, and the way you live, how often can you tell what's genuinely yours and what's been shaped by your upbringing or other people's expectations?

options.label: I rarely question it. I mostly live what was expected.
I can see some of it, usually after the fact.
I can tell the difference in some areas.
I can usually feel what is mine and what has been shaped by my upbringing or other people's expectations.
I can see it clearly and choose what is genuinely mine.

questions.prompt: When the same kind of problem keeps showing up in your work, relationships, or life, where does your attention usually go first?

options.label: To what other people are doing wrong.
Mostly to blame, frustration, or defending myself.
Some blame, some self-inquiry.
To what is mine to see or change.
To what is mine, what is theirs, and what reality is showing me.

questions.prompt: When you stop doing, achieving and looking after everyone else, how comfortable are you simply being yourself?

options.label: Not comfortable. I don't really know who I am without doing.
I get glimpses, but I don't stay there for long.
I'm somewhat comfortable simply being myself.
I'm mostly comfortable simply being myself.
I don't need achievement or performance to know who I am.

questions.prompt: When someone criticises you, rejects you, or says something that triggers you, how often can you stay with what you're feeling before reacting?

options.label: I react almost immediately.
I usually react first and realise later.
I sometimes catch myself before reacting.
I can usually stay with what I'm feeling before I respond.
I can stay present with what I'm feeling and choose my response.

questions.prompt: Before a difficult conversation, an important decision, or a meeting you're dreading, how often do you notice what's happening in your body?

options.label: Rarely. I push straight through.
Occasionally.
Sometimes.
Often.
Consistently. I trust what my body is telling me and include it in my decisions.

questions.prompt: When you can tell something is off with someone close to you, what do you usually do?

options.label: I don't usually notice it, or I ignore it.
I notice it, but avoid bringing it up.
I usually assume I know what's going on.
I stay curious and ask instead of assuming.
I ask, stay curious, and let them have their own experience.

questions.prompt: When you're being challenged, criticised, or all eyes are on you, how much of your attention goes into managing how you come across instead of simply being yourself?

options.label: Almost all of my attention goes into managing how I come across.
I spend more time managing myself than simply being myself.
It's about half and half.
I'm mostly myself, even under pressure.
I can stay fully myself, even when I'm being watched, challenged or judged.

questions.prompt: When you look at what you want most in your life right now, how much of it comes from genuine desire rather than trying to prove something?

options.label: Almost all of it is about proving something.
More of it comes from proving than genuine desire.
It's about half and half.
Most of it comes from genuine desire.
I can feel the difference and choose what is genuinely true for me.

questions.prompt: Over the past month, how much of your time, attention and energy has gone towards what you genuinely want, rather than what you feel you should be doing?

options.label: Very little. Most of my time went towards what I felt I should be doing.
Some, but "should" still drives more of my life than what I genuinely want.
It's about half and half.
Most of my time, attention and energy goes towards what I genuinely want.
My life is increasingly organised around what I genuinely want, even when it asks more of me than feels comfortable.

questions.prompt: When you find yourself complaining about your work, a relationship, your health, or your life, what do you usually do with that complaint?

options.label: I keep complaining, but nothing really changes.
I vent, feel better for a while, then end up back in the same place.
I sometimes realise my complaint is pointing to something deeper.
I usually use my complaint to discover what I really want or need to face.
I know that behind every complaint is a hidden desire, and I use it to guide my next step.

questions.prompt: When life doesn't go the way you planned, what usually happens to what you genuinely want?

options.label: I lose sight of what I want and go into survival mode.
I hold on tightly to the original plan, even when it's no longer working.
I adapt, but it feels like I've lost something important.
I stay connected to what really matters and find another way forward.
Life often helps me discover an even more honest version of what I truly want.

questions.prompt: Life moves fast. Between work, other people's needs and your own ambitions, how much of your life do you actually meet as it's happening, rather than being dragged through it or watching it go by?

options.label: Most of the time I'm dragged along or checked out. Life is happening, but I'm not really in it.
I catch brief moments of feeling fully engaged, but they don't last.
I'm engaged with parts of my life, but a lot of it still passes me by.
I'm engaged with most of my life as it happens, not just the parts that are going well.
I meet life as it's happening, moment to moment. Engaged, not dragged, not watching from the sidelines.

questions.prompt: When you look back on a typical week, which of these feels most true?

options.label: I mostly get through the week.
I achieve a lot, but still feel strangely empty, like I can't quite touch the deepest part of myself.
Some moments feel meaningful, but I know there's something more available to me.
Most weeks feel meaningful and aligned with who I am.
By the end of most weeks, what I did and what I actually wanted were the same thing. I feel well used, not used up.

questions.prompt: What usually happens to the difficult experiences, emotions, and conversations that remain unfinished in your life?

options.label: I mostly carry them with me. They build up over time.
I try to move on, but they often stay with me longer than I'd like.
I work through some of them, but others keep resurfacing.
I usually find healthy ways to process and complete them.
I have reliable practices that help me complete experiences, return to myself, and stay engaged with life.
